title: The Macro Minute: How soon until the Fed is providing liquidity via QE?

Happy Thursday out there at team42. It's
your skipper here, Darius delivers in
our macro minute for Thursday, March
20th, 2025. As always, we'll start with
the executive summary from today's
leadoff morning note. If you would like
to review the analysis supporting these
conclusions and to see if you need to
make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk,
you need to be a client of 42 macro. So
today's key macro question is how soon
until the Fed is providing liquidity via
QE. Our answers are at yesterday's FOMC,
the Fed voted to maintain the benchmark
federal funds rate uh to for at 4.5% and
slow the pace of balance sheet
reduction. The Fed lowered its growth
forecast, raised inflation estimates,
increased its unemployment rate forecast
and maintained projections for two 25
basis point rate cuts this year, but
chair pow stating that lower growth and
higher inflation balanced out in policy
considerations. Starting in April, the
Fed will reduce the cap on Treasury
maturities, rolling off its balance
sheet from 25 billion to 5 billion per
month to mitigate strain while keeping
mortgage back securities cap at 35
billion per month. We've consistently
reiterated for months that the Fed went
in the Treasury portion of balance sheet
runoff in the first half of 2025.
Yesterday's reduction only a 5 billion a
month pace is a massive step in that
direction. Yesterday's bullish response
across asset markets was appropriate. If
our medium-term fundamental outlook is
reasonably accurate, the risk asset
rally will likely fade within a month
because ending fake QT is not the same
as implementing QE. The lack of
follow-through today is supportive of
this view. Both POW and Fed Governor
Michelle Bowman recently advocated for
adjusting the supplemental liquidity
ratio or supplemental leverage ratio
rather SLR regulations to improve uh
treasury market liquidity and to address
unintended regulatory consequences. In
yesterday's all-in podcast with Jamaat
Palapatia and David Friedberg, Treasury
Secretarius Scott Bessence confirmed the
Trump administration is also on board
with the reduction or abolition of the
SLR. That is great news for investors
because conservative estimates of its
impact suggest it could free up an
additional two to 400 billion in bank
capital for treasuries or repo which
will be an indirect indirect source of
incremental treasury demand. Reminder,
asset markets need QE in 2025 to prevent
a global debt refinancing air pocket and
they will eventually receive it. If
however QE is not supplied on a
proactive enough basis, it is not likely
risk assets have formed a durable
bottom. Scribbing the SLR would be a big
nice step in the right direction and may
limit downside and risk assets over the
medium term. Be careful not to overreact
to that statement as limiting downside
is not the same as forming a durable
bottom. Traders, trade. Investors, be
patient. Know your role. And most
importantly, know your financial goals.
You don't have to trade every wiggle to
in the stock market or in the crypto
market to retire on time and
comfortably. 42 macro's large and
glowing client base is living proof of
that. Transition to our 42 micro
dashboard. Uh this is always a wrap up
with the question from our client base.
This one's regarding bonds. Says, "Hi,
Skipper. Using the 10-year bond yield
components as a proxy. I see the real
yield and term premium components have
contracted this year while the 10-year
break even rate is flattish. I have just
a few questions regarding these
components. Do you think term premium
will largely be ignored until we get
closer to the debt ceiling becoming an
issue again in the third quarter of
2025? Will the growth scare policy
uncertainty from this point be enough to
push real yields down even further?
especially given that markets are
already pricing in more rate cuts than
what the Fed has articulated and then
given that the Fed is viewing tariff
policy uh tariff impact as a transitory
uh one-off shock to short-term inflation
prospects will a sequential upside
surprises CPI and BC from here be enough
to push the 10-year break even rate
higher. So uh let me um let me unpack
each of those in succession. So starting
with do you think term premier will be
largely ignored until we get closer to
the debt sealing impass become an issue
again? Uh so we talked about yesterday
how uh we essentially expect the uh debt
limit x date the default date which
they're not going to default but uh the
the the projected default date is
somewhere between mid June and late
August. It's hard to be clear uh until
we get to the other side of um tax
season individual uh income tax season
in in late April in mid to late April.
So uh assuming that uh term premium in
our opinion was is likely to widen once
the market gets a bit more clarity on
the contours of the um of the fiscal
policy agenda specifically as relates to
extending and expanding the Trump tax
cuts and jobs act. There's still some
sort of um fluctuations that they can
do. There's still some some levers that
they can pull to make that package
either uh they can make the package
bigger on the tax cut side if they find
offsetting uh um offsetting cuts. Um and
so um We we'll see if they can kind of
what the ultimate size and scope of the
package is. But the number one thing
that I've been focused on, we've been
focused on here in our research is
whether or not uh they use the current
policy or current law baseline. If they
use the current law baseline, then
they're going to, you know, they
basically only have enough money to kind
of extend the Trump tax cuts and maybe
add this or that there at the margins.
But if they use the current policy
baseline, then they can throw in a whole
range of goodies uh as it relates to
President Trump's campaign promises on
the tax relief side. So, uh, if they go
down this current pol current current
policy baseline, you're going to see a
much bigger, uh, a much larger hit, um,
much larger increase in structural
treasury supply that's added to the
market. In our opinion, we think that's
when term premium will begin to rise
again as a function of that. So, it's
really just a function of that that
really critical choice that they have to
make uh, in Congress. And we ultimately
think that based on, you know, President
Trump's dominance of the Republican
party and every and how everyone's
really fallen in line with the passage
of the uh of the continued resolution,
with the passage of the reconciliation
instructions, and obviously going back
to February with the passage of the the
actual um budget proposal, you know,
it's very clear that President Trump is
the party's whip right now and and it's
working. And so, it's our belief that
there probably are going to go down the
current policy baseline and ultimately
we're going to see a much larger tax
package. uh from that perspective. Um so
that will be negative from a turn frame
perspective but obviously positive uh
from the for the structural growth
outlook. Um so that's keep that in mind.
Uh will a growth scare policy
uncertainty from this point be enough to
push real yields down even further
especially given that markets are
already pricing in more rate cuts than
what the Fed uh have are articulated? Uh
the answer is yeah absolutely. Uh so you
know right now if you look at where
consensus GDP estimates are or where
consensus sales and earnings forecasts
are for S&P companies S&P 500 companies
they barely budged. And so it's our
belief that if we have a further a
faster than expected slowdown in growth
and obviously the Fed is kind of now you
know I would say leading the charge.
We're you know we we're leading the
charge. The Fed is following us and we
think consensus investor consensus will
follow the Fed in the coming weeks and
months. If growth slows faster than than
than what they're currently expecting,
then the market will price in more rate
cuts, it's not about what the Fed wants
to happen. It's about what the market is
willing to price in as a function of
that. Uh so, and again, we could be
wrong on all this, but if we're right,
then that what's likely to happen is the
market will price more rate cuts in than
two uh really, it's really it's two and
a half currently. Uh and they'll, you
know, so it could go down as much as
probably four uh uh this year because
ultimately it'll think the Fed's behind
the curve in that scenario. So you can
definitely see real yields lower from
here in a growth in a true legitimate
growth scare which we continue to
believe is underappreciated by markets
uh uh by asset markets. And again we
could be wrong in everything we're
saying by the way. You know it's not
like you know we have some crystal ball
here. We're just doing you know dozens
of slides of research you know in our
leadoff warning note every day. We're
doing you know 150 plus slides of
research in our macro scouting report
every month you know to arrive at these
conclusions. And those conclusions could
be wrong. But again you know we we I
think we're pretty good at what we do
here. uh you know sorry I know we're
pretty good at what we're do here so I
have a lot of confidence in our in our
forecast relative to most but uh getting
back into this you know could the market
um price in more rate cuts the Fed has
articulated absolutely it will it will
certainly will if if growth slows faster
than what people are expecting and we
believe that it it is likely to you know
again we have this significant fiscal
retrenchment coming from Doge coming
from the budget process as well as the
the acquisition the the application of
tariffs which is a statation ary shock
to the economy. People have already
forgotten about the uh uh the
significant slowdown in immigration
where you know if you look at u border
crossings they're down 88% in February.
They were down 66% in January. You know
basically you were basically reduced
immigration to zero essentially. I mean
not zero but you know essentially. And
so that's going to slow labor supply
growth. That's going to slow job
creation. That's going to ultimately
slow the rate of of of consumer income
growth. And so that's another headwind.
And then you also just have steepening
base effects from two plus years of real
final sales growing at or above 3%. You
know, so that's a that's a tough comp to
begin with. So you have a lot of
negative things happening, not the least
of which is the one-off, you know, kind
of stagflationary shock to the economy
from the acquisition of the imposition
of tariffs. So there's a lot of negative
stuff happening over the next, you know,
let's call it two to maybe three
quarters that has not yet been reflected
in GDP estimates or consensus estimates
for sales and earnings on the micro
level. So that in our opinion is a big
market risk. Obviously on the other side
of that, you're going to get the QE if
we're right. You're going to get the tax
cuts, you're going to get the
deregulation. So you're going to want to
buy that dip if we continue to dip from
here. Uh but ultimately uh we think, you
know, it's it's a bit premature. Uh even
if we're in a multi-week rally because
that's what happens in bare markets. You
have these multi-week uh rallies. So
don't get try not to get head faked if
if you're out there. Uh that's one of
our key takeaways from yesterday's macro
minute. And then finally, uh, wrapping
up, given that the Fed is viewing the
tariff impact as transitory as a
transitory one-off shock to short-term
inflation prospects, which is true.
That's how tariffs have historically uh
filled through the economy, uh, will
sequential upside and CBI or PC from
here be enough to push the 10-year break
even inflate higher? No. The 10ear break
even inflation rate is highly correlated
with growth expectations, highly
correlated with stock prices, highly
correlated with forward expectations of
earnings. If if we have a faster than
expected growth slowdown, you're going
to see the 10-year break even rate
decline. even if you know it's really
not uh even if it's even if it's a
misnomer uh even if it's not really true
uh even if long-term inflation prospects
are not materially being altered that's
just how the break even market is
historically traded and we're not
expecting a material departure from how
it's historically traded. So uh in terms
of bond yields, I think what's more
likely to happen from here is we
probably get a growth proper growth
scare, not not this kind of two week
growth scare that we've had thus far or
not I don't want to be
dismissive, you know, kind of one month
growth scare that we've had this far. We
think that's going to deepen. Uh if that
deepens, we're going to see bond yields
lower. Uh eventually bond yields will
find a bottom once they start to get
credible signs from policy makers that
the Fed put andor the Trump put has been
achieved. and then you buy the dip and
you it's off to the races on both bond
yields and risk asset prices terms. So,
you know, we continue to view 1998 as a
very legitimate market analog for 2025.
And again, like I keep saying, we could
be wrong about all this. The market
could have bottomed already. We could be
finishing the year at 7,000 S&P and
150,000 Bitcoin. and and that maybe very
well be true, but based on everything we
think we know as a function of
